Superior corrosion resistance and its origins in an additively manufactured Co-Cr-Ni-Al-Ti high-entropy alloy with nano-lamellar precipitates

A B S T R A C T

$ L_{12} $-strengthened high-entropy alloys (HEAs) are promising materials for advanced structural applications in harsh environments due to their outstanding mechanical properties. However, the Cr-depleted  $ L_{12} $ particles usually increase the alloy's galvanic corrosion susceptibility, thus resulting in a decrease in pitting resistance. In this work, the corrosion behavior and associated passive film characteristics of a novel  $ Co_{40}Cr_{20}Ni_{30}Al_{5}Ti_{5} $ HEA (at.%) additively manufactured by selective laser melting (SLM) were systematically characterized and investigated. It was found that the precipitation of coherent nano-lamellar  $ L_{12} $ phase significantly refined the grain structure of the aged alloy, which leads to an anomalously improved corrosion resistance compared to the as-printed single-phase counterpart. Such excellent corrosion resistance of the aged alloy originated from the thin amorphous passive film with  $ Cr_{2}O_{3} $,  $ Al_{2}O_{3} $, and  $ TiO_{2} $ as the main constituents that were firmly adhered to the alloy matrix. Moreover, the corrosion morphologies revealed that the dense and large-sized pits on the as-printed alloy were in sharp contrast to the sparse and irregularly-shaped pits on the aged alloy, which can be attributed to the potential difference and/or the refined grain structure. These findings will effectively advance the development of corrosion-resistant additively manufactured alloys and provide new insights into the innovative design of high-performance damage-tolerant  $ L_{12} $-strengthened HEAs.

1. Introduction

Over the past decade, there have been and currently are still ongoing significant research and industrial strives to develop and manufacture advanced metallic materials via additive manufacturing, which hold great promise for structural, functional, and engineering applications [1–4]. Additive manufacturing, as a bottom-up technique, has obvious advantages over traditional subtractive methods in manufacturing complex-shaped structural parts, not to mention its efficient material usage [1]. As of now, additive manufacturing has made great progress in the fabrication of traditional single-component alloy systems, such as Fe-based stainless steel (SS) [2,5], Ni-based alloys [6], Ti alloys [3,7], Al alloys [8], Mg alloys [9], and Cu alloys [10] regarding good comprehensive properties (mechanical and corrosion properties). In particular, taking the additively manufactured 316 L SS as an example, it not only possesses superior mechanical properties to its conventional wrought counterpart, but its corrosion resistance, especially pitting resistance, is also significantly improved, which is attributed to the unique microstructure and the reduction of MnS inclusions caused by the thermal distortions and rapid cooling during the additive manufacturing process [11–13]. Similar corrosion resistance improvements were also observed in additively manufactured 17–4PH martensitic

SS [14]. These exciting advances are also driving the exploration of additively manufactured multi-component high-entropy alloys (HEAs).

The emerging HEAs with widely tunable compositions represent a new alloy design concept, which breaks through the conventional alloy design strategies based on the single-principle element and expands the alloy design repertoire for more possible alloying element combinations [15]. In recent years, some representative HEAs via additive manufacturing have been successfully prepared and exhibited satisfactory comprehensive mechanical properties, such as Cantor alloy [16,17],  $ Fe_{50}Mn_{30}Co_{10}Cr_{10} $ dual-phase alloy [18], equiatomic FeNiCr alloy [19],  $ L1_{2} $-strengthed ( $ CoCrNi)_{94}Al_{3}Ti_{3} $ alloy [20],  $ L1_{2} $-strengthed ( $ FeCoNi)_{86}Al_{7}Ti_{7} $ alloy [21],  $ AlCoCrFeNi_{2.1} $ eutectic HEA [22], refractory HEAs [23], etc. Among them, the  $ L1_{2} $-strengthened HEAs have been demonstrated to exhibit outstanding mechanical properties, not only for arc-melted alloys but also for additively manufactured counterparts [20,24,25]. However, apart from many detailed investigations of their mechanical behavior, the corrosion properties of  $ L1_{2} $-strengthened HEAs have yet to be thoroughly studied, especially for the corrosion of additively manufactured  $ L1_{2} $-strengthened HEAs [26]. A very recent study showed that the aging heat treatment improved the corrosion resistance of additively manufactured  $ L1_{2} $-strengthened ( $ FeCoNi)_{86}Al_{7}Ti_{7} $ HEA, but the lack of Cr (an important passivating element) and the presence of many Heusler particles in this alloy resulted in unsatisfactory anti-corrosion performance both in the as-printed and age-treated state [27]. Admittedly, the compositional inhomogeneities due to the  $ L1_{2} $ precipitation coupled with the intrinsic elemental segregation (usually at the cellular boundaries) of the additively manufactured microstructures poses a significant challenge to the corrosion resistance of  $ L1_{2} $-strengthened HEAs fabricated by additive manufacturing. In our previous study, we found that the  $ Co_{40}Cr_{20}Ni_{30}Al_{5}Ti_{5} $ (designated as the Al5Ti5 hereafter) HEA had superior corrosion resistance than that of the traditional 304 SS in both single-phase FCC structure and  $ FCC+L1_{2} $ dual-phase structure, even though the presence of Cr-depleted  $ L1_{2} $ particles slightly reduced the alloy's localized corrosion resistance [28]. Therefore, in the corresponding additively manufactured Al5Ti5 HEA, the corrosion behavior in the as-printed state and the effect of  $ L1_{2} $ precipitation on its corrosion resistance are worthy of in-depth and systematic studies, especially considering the unique microstructure imparted by additive manufacturing.

In this study, a novel additively manufactured Al5Ti5 HEA was first successfully prepared using the SLM technique. The aging heat treatment was carefully tailored to introduce the L1₂ nanoprecipitates. Subsequently, the detailed microstructures and compositional distributions of the additively manufactured Al5Ti5 HEA in the as-printed and aged states were carefully studied using multi-scale characterization methods. The electrochemical corrosion behavior and passivation characteristics of the two alloys were investigated by potentiodynamic and potentiostatic polarization tests. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were used to further investigate the electrochemical stability of the passive films on the surfaces of both alloys. The chemical state analysis of the passive film and the proportion of individual oxides or hydroxides were achieved by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tests. The lateral chemical distribution and structural aspects of the passive film on the L1₂-strengthened aged alloy were characterized by scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mapping and atomic-resolution high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) imaging. Finally, the different corrosion morphologies caused by the unique microstructures of the as-printed and aged alloys were characterized by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), which was also carefully discussed. We expect that the present work represents future research direction towards corrosion-resistant additively manufactured HEAs and will inspire waves of efforts to develop high-performance L1₂-strengthened HEAs for advanced structural applications in aggressive environments.
2. Experimental procedure
2.1. Alloy fabrication

The gas-atomized Al5Ti5 HEA powders with an average particle size of 36.36  $ \mu $m were used to fabricate Al5Ti5 bulk specimens. The SLM was performed using a laser powder-bed-fusion (LPBF) printer (SLM-100, Guangdong Hanbang) under an argon atmosphere with optimized printing parameters (laser power: 175 W, scan speed: 1000 mm/s, layer thickness: 30  $ \mu $m, hatching space: 80  $ \mu $m). Subsequently, the as-printed specimen was aged at 700 °C for 4 h to introduce the second reinforced phase, i.e., the L12 nanoprecipitates. The aging heat treatment ended with air cooling. Small discs measuring 14 mm (diameter) and 5 mm (thickness) were cut from the as-printed and aged rectangular plates for the subsequent electrochemical testing. The test surface (X-Y plane) was perpendicular to the building direction and away from the printing substrate.
2.2. Microstructure observations

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were collected from the mechanically polished specimen surface by an XRD (SmartLab, Rigaku) device using a monochromatic Cu Kα radiation (wavelength = 1.54 Å). The diffraction patterns were first recorded over a 2θ range from 20° to 100° at a scanning speed of 5°/min and a step size of 0.5°. Then, the scanning step size was set to 0.05° to detect (100) and (110) peaks in the range of 20°–40° and the (220) peak in the range of 72°–78° for the aged alloy to verify the presence of L1₂ phase. The lattice mismatch of the aged alloy was calculated from the deconvolution results of the (220) diffraction peak according to  $ \delta = 2(a_{L1_2} - a_{FCC})/(a_{L1_2} + a_{FCC}) $, where  $ a_{L1_2} $ and  $ a_{FCC} $ are the lattice constants of the L1₂ phase and FCC matrix, respectively. Meso-scale microstructural analyses were performed on a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Quanta FEG450, FEI) equipped with an electron back-scattered diffraction (EBSD, EDAX Velocity, GATAN) detector. Specimens for SEM and EBSD observations were prepared by standard metallographic procedures, including grinding with a series of SiC abrasive papers and mechanical polishing using a diamond suspension with a particle size of 1 μm. Afterwards, the electrolytic polishing was performed in a nitric acid-alcohol mixed solution (nitric acid:alcohol = 1:3) at a cryogenic temperature to obtain a high surface quality. The micro/nanoscale microstructural analyses were performed with the aid of a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, 2100F, JEOL) and a STEM (Talos F200X, FEI) equipped with a Super-X energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) detector. Specimens for TEM and STEM observations were prepared by cutting, grinding, dimpling, and finally argon ion thinning. Near-atomic scale compositional distributions were obtained by atom probe tomography (APT) testing in a local electrode atom probe (LEAP, 5000XR, CAMECA) under high vacuum (10⁻⁸ Torr) and cryogenic conditions (77 K). Needle-shaped specimens for APT testing were prepared by the usual lift-out procedure in a focused ion beam/scanning electron microscope (FIB/SEM, Scios 2, FEI) dual-beam system. To minimize the Ga⁺ implantation into the specimens, the final polishing was carried out sequentially at 5 kV/48 pA and 2 kV/27 pA.
2.3. Electrochemical tests
The room-temperature electrochemical tests were carried out in a 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution, which was prepared from the reagent-grade NaCl and deionized water with a resistivity of  $ 18.2 M\Omega $.

A conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell containing a working electrode (the targeted alloys), a reference electrode (a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) connected to the electrolyte with a Luggin capillary), and a counter electrode (a platinum foil) was used to perform the electrochemical testing. The electrochemical data were collected on a potentiostat (CHI 760E). The surfaces of the specimens used for electrochemical testing were ground to 5000-grit, then mirror polished using a 1-μm suspension before being ultrasonicated in alcohol for 5 min, and finally dried in the flowing cold air. The specimen surface exposed to the corrosive solution was defined as 1 cm² by a rubber O-ring (inert to the chemical reaction). After the specimen was exposed to the solution, it was first cathodically polarized at -0.8 V $ _{SCE} $ for 5 min to minimize the effects of the air-formed oxide. Subsequently, the open-circuit potential (OCP) was recorded for 3600 s before EIS testing. A frequency range of 10⁵ to 10⁻² Hz and an excitation voltage of 10 mV were applied during the EIS measurements. The resistivity profile and other physical properties, such as the effective capacitance of passive films, were calculated and analyzed based on the fitting results of the impedance spectra with ZView software. The potentiodynamic polarization tests were performed with a scan rate of 0.1 mV/s from -0.6 V $ _{SCE} $ towards the anodic direction to characterize the formation and breakdown behavior of the passive film. In addition, in order to compare the corrosion morphology differences between the as-printed and aged alloys, some specimens were subjected to a constant potential at 1 V $ _{SCE} $ (above the E $ _{pit} $) for 120 s. The corrosion morphologies were then characterized by SEM and the CLSM (VKX3000, Keyence). All electrochemical tests were repeated at least three times to ensure the data reproducibility.
2.4. Passive film characterization

The passivation-induced surface films that were obtained by potentiostatic polarization testing at 0.15 V $ _{SCE} $ (in their passive region) for 7200 s on the as-printed and aged alloys were subjected to XPS analysis. After the passivation was completed, the specimens were immediately removed from the solution, rinsed briefly with deionized water to remove any electrolyte residues, and then dried under the flowing nitrogen gas. A portable vacuum container was used to transfer the passivated specimens to the XPS vacuum transfer chamber to minimize air exposure. XPS characterization was carried out on a Thermo Scientific Escalab Xi+ spectrometer with a monochromatized Al K $ \alpha $ X-ray source (1486.6 eV), and the analysis chamber was maintained at a pressure of  $ \sim10^{-8} $ Torr during the characterizations. The survey spectrum was first acquired at a pass energy of 200 eV to identify the possible existing chemical species. Subsequently, the high-resolution spectra of O 1s, C 1s, Co 2p, Cr 2p, Ni 2p, Al 2p, and Ti 2p were obtained at 20 eV pass energy in steps of 0.1 eV. Four random positions of each specimen were selected for the XPS testing. Data processing (peak fitting and deconvolution) was performed using the Thermo Avantage software. All XPS spectra were referenced to the adventitious alkyl (C–C) peak with a binding energy of 284.8 eV. The high-resolution core level spectra of each element were fitted based on the Gaussian/Lorentzian product function (65% Gaussian, 35% Lorentzian) and the known reference spectra after iterative Shirley-type background subtraction. The characterization of the elemental distribution across the lateral passive film, as well as the correlated structural features, were performed on a dual spherical aberration-corrected (scanning) TEM (Titan Cubed Themis G2 300, FEI) at an accelerating voltage of 300 kV. The cross-sectional thin foil of the passive film was fabricated on an FEI FIB/SEM dual-beam system using the lift-out method. Ga $ ^{+} $ was used as the ion source. To protect the passive film from Ga $ ^{+} $ damage, a thin Au layer was previously coated onto the specimen surface using a vacuum sputtering device (Smart Coater, JEOL). After FIB milling, the thin slice sample was finally thinned and polished by the low-energy (ion source energy: 900 eV) and concentrated (emission current: 150  $ \mu $A) argon ion beam milling equipment (NanoMill, Model 1040, Fischione) for 20 min.

Fig. 2 shows the meso-scale to micro-scale initial microstructure of the as-printed alloy. The inverse pole figure (IPF) image (Fig. 2(a)) first shows that the as-printed alloy has a conventional polycrystalline morphology with an average grain size of 29.7  $ \mu $m (inset in Fig. 2(a)). The corresponding kernel average misorientation (KAM) map (Fig. 2(b)) shows that a significant local misorientation exists in the as-printed alloy, and its average KAM value is 0.85° (inset in Fig. 2(b)), suggesting that it contains a relatively high dislocation density. The representative SEM image (Fig. 2(c)) reveals that cellular structures with an average size of  $ \sim $400 nm prevail inside the grains. This kind of cellular structure was reported to be caused by thermal distortion during the printing process, which has also been widely observed in additively manufactured 316 L SS [29], Al-based alloys [8], and Ni-based alloys [30], Co-based alloys [31]. The bright-field TEM image (Fig. 2(d)) revealed that the cellular walls were decorated with a high density of dislocations, with relatively few dislocations inside. The selected-area diffraction pattern (SADP, inset in Fig. 2(d)) along the [011] zone axis once again verifies the single-phase FCC structure of the as-printed alloy, without any other superlattice spots. Further STEM image and corresponding EDS maps (Fig. 2(e)) show that the cellular walls are obviously enriched in Ti and slightly depleted in Co and Cr. Ni and Al show no significant partitioning behavior at the cellular walls, as evidenced by the EDS line profiles in Fig. 2(f).

The initial microstructure characterization of the aged alloy is shown in Fig. 3. Compared with the as-printed alloy, a notable change is that the average grain size of the aged alloy is greatly reduced to 9.2  $ \mu $m, and the average KAM value is also reduced to 0.67°, which are reflected in Fig. 3(a, b), respectively. The alleviation of local misorientations (KAM value) is apparently due to the partial annihilation of dislocations caused by the aging heat treatment. The reduced grain size, on the other hand, may be due to the discontinuous precipitation of the L1₂ phase, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The migrating grain boundary indicated by the yellow dashed line was used to distinguish between the continuous precipitation (CP) and discontinuous precipitation (DP) regions. The cellular structures can still be observed in the CP region but not in the DP region. Instead, a stripe-shaped contrast difference appears in the DP region. This microstructural difference can be further observed in the magnified images of the DP (Fig. 3(d)) and CP regions (Fig. 3(e)). 3.2. Overview of electrochemical behavior
3.2.1. Potentiodynamic and potentiostatic polarization tests

Fig. 6(a) shows the potentiodynamic traces of the as-printed and aged Al5Ti5 alloys recorded in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solution. Both alloys exhibit spontaneous passivity with no active-to-passive transition. It is apparent that the anodic current density of the aged alloy is lower than that of the as-printed counterpart at each potential. The key electrochemical parameters obtained from the traces are tabulated in Table 2. From these data, it can be found that the corrosion potential ( $ E_{corr} $) and pitting potential ( $ E_{pit} $) of the aged alloy are increased to -311 mV $ _{SCE} $ and 477 mV $ _{SCE} $, respectively, compared to those of the as-printed alloys. Higher  $ E_{corr} $ and  $ E_{pit} $ (or  $ \Delta E $) indicate that the aged Al5Ti5 alloy has better comprehensive corrosion performance in aggressive saline solutions, including superior general corrosion resistance and localized corrosion resistance. Moreover, the passivation current density ( $ i_{pass} $) of the aged alloy shows a slight decrease compared to the as-printed alloy, suggesting that the passive films formed on the aged alloy during anodic passivation are more electrochemically stable. This is also reflected in the potentiostatic traces (Fig. 6(b)) of the two alloys, where the selected applied potential lies within their passive region. It can be further observed from Fig. 6(b) that the logarithmic current density decreases linearly with the logarithmic passivation time, and the current density of the aged alloy is continuously lower than that of the as-printed alloy, which indicates that (i) both alloys can quickly form stable passive films at this chosen potential (150 mV $ _{SCE} $), and (ii) for the aged alloy, the passive film is much more stable. These results demonstrate that the aged alloy containing the L12 phase tends to form a more stable and robust passive film in dilute chloride solution than the as-printed alloy, thus exhibiting superior corrosion resistance.

The truncated capacitive arcs are observed on both alloys, which is a typical feature of the passive materials, suggesting a deviation of the passive films from the ideal capacitive behavior [32–34]. In addition, the aged alloy has a larger capacitive arc radius than the as-printed alloy, indicating a larger impedance value of the surface passive film, which means that the passive film on aged alloys is electrochemically more stable [33]. This is also reflected in the diagram drawn in the Bode coordinates, as displayed in Fig. 7(b), containing both variations of impedance modulus ( $ [Z] $) and phase angle ( $ \theta $) with respect to the frequency (f). Specifically, in the low-frequency range (from  $ 10^{-2} $ to  $ 10^{-1} $ Hz),  $ [Z] $ is generally proportional to the robustness of the passive film [35]. The higher  $ [Z] $ of the aged alloy confirms its superior corrosion resistance, which is consistent with the results of the potentiodynamic and potentiostatic polarization tests. In the intermediate-frequency range (from  $ 10^{0} $ to  $ 10^{3} $ Hz), a straight line with a slope of  $ \sim0.94 $ is observed in the plot of  $ \log|Z| $ vs.  $ \log(f) $ and a clear plateau is observed in

Moreover, the impedance response in the high-frequency interval (from  $ 10^{4} $ to  $ 10^{5} $ Hz) usually reflects the characteristics of the solution resistance, as manifested by the observed very low  $ [Z] $ and the near-zero  $ \theta $.

To better understand the physical properties of passive films and explain the non-ideal capacitive behavior, the Young model and the power-law model based on the distribution of electrical properties (resistivity or conductivity) are considered two promising fitting models for impedance data [37]. The non-stoichiometric nature of the passive film leads to an exponential or power-law distribution of the local resistivity normal to the alloy surface, which corresponds to the Young model [38,39] and the power-law model [40,41], respectively. Given that both models can provide physically reasonable interpretations for passive films, they have been widely used to explain the impedance data on Fe-based stainless steels [35,42,43] and the more recent HEAs [28,44]. The resistivity distributions of the passive films described in the two models are derived from the Voigt measurement model proposed by Agarwal et al. [45,46], shown in Fig. 7(c). The Voigt circuit contains multiple Voigt elements in series with a solution resistance ( $ R_{0} $), where  $ R_{i} $ and  $ C_{i} $ are the local resistance and capacitance, respectively. The Voigt elements were successively added to the measurement model until further additions no longer improved the fitting quality, and regression parameters containing zero within their 95.4% ( $ \pm 2\sigma $) confidence interval were not adopted [40]. The fitting results obtained by regression of the Voigt measurement model are shown by the solid lines in Fig. 7 (a, b).

Each time constant is assumed to be related to the differential layer of the passive film. The local capacitance is related to the thickness of each Voigt element  $ i $ ( $ d_i $) and the local dielectric constant ( $ \varepsilon_i $), i.e.,  $ C_i = \frac{\varepsilon_i \varepsilon_0}{d_i} $, where  $ \varepsilon_0 $ is the vacuum permittivity of  $ 8.85 \times 10^{-14} $ F/cm. Considering a uniformly distributed dielectric constant, the local resistivity ( $ \rho_i = \frac{\tau_i}{\varepsilon \cdot \varepsilon_0} $) can be obtained [40].  $ \varepsilon $ is the dielectric constant of the passive film. The dielectric constants of the passive films on the surfaces of the as-printed and aged alloys were determined by the subsequent XPS fitting results and the mixture rule ( $ \ln \varepsilon = \sum V_x \ln \varepsilon_x $) [47,48].  $ V_x $ and  $ \varepsilon_x $ are the volume fraction and dielectric constant of the corresponding component oxides, respectively. Since the passive films of both alloys are dominated by  $ Cr_2O_3 $ ( $ \varepsilon_{Cr_2O_3} = 25 $) [49,50],  $ Al_2O_3 $ ( $ \varepsilon_{Al_2O_3} = 9 $) [51,52], and  $ TiO_2 $ ( $ \varepsilon_{TiO_2} = 60 $) [53] (as we will demonstrate in the XPS section), their dielectric constants were calculated to be roughly the same, about 24.1. Therefore, the local resistivity of the passive films can be calculated. The dimensionless resistivity ( $ \rho/\rho_\delta $) is then plotted as a function of dimensionless position ( $ \xi $,  $ \xi = x/\delta $), as shown in Fig. 7(d).  $ \delta $ is the thickness of the passive film ( $ \delta = n \frac{d_i}{i=1} $),  $ \rho_\delta $ denotes the resistivity boundary value at the solution/passive film interface ( $ \xi = 1 $) [54]. As can be seen from Fig. 7(d), the dimensionless resistivity follows an approximately linear profile in the semilogarithmic coordinates, indicating that the local resistivity exhibits an exponential distribution with respect to the normal distance to both as-printed and aged alloys, which is in good agreement with the Young model [35]. The nonlinear relationship between the dimensionless resistivity and dimensionless position on a logarithmic scale (Fig. S3) further suggests that the local resistivity distribution coincides with an exponential rather than a power-law resistivity profile [39]. This scenario where the passive film can be well-described by the Young model is also observed on the surface passive films of additively manufactured 316 SS [35] and 17–4PH SS [42].

3.3. XPS characterization of passive films

It is well documented that the electrochemical corrosion behavior and associated corrosion resistance are determined by the nature of the nanoscale protective (or passive) film on the alloy surface [58–60]. Given the high surface detection sensitivity of XPS, we can provide the quantitative chemical state analysis of passive films [61–63]. Fig. 8 illustrates the high-resolution spectra recorded in the Co 2p₃/₂, Cr 2p₃/₂, Ni 2p₃/₂, Al 2p, Ti 2p₃/₂, and O 1s regions of the as-printed alloy surface after being held at 0.15 VSCE in 3.5 wt.% NaCl for 7200 s. The Co, Cr, and Ni signals all originated from the three chemical states of respective elements: the metallic state (Co⁰, Cr⁰, and Ni⁰) with a lower binding energy, the oxide state (Co₃O₄, Cr₂O₃, and NiO) in the middle binding energy, and the hydroxide state (Co(OH)₂, Cr(OH)₃, and Ni(OH)₂) characterized by a higher binding energy. Co⁰ and Ni⁰ are the main constituent species of the Co 2p₃/₂ and the Ni 2p₃/₂ spectrum, respectively, while Cr₂O₃ accounts for the main part of the Cr 2p₃/₂ spectrum. The Al and Ti signals both originated from two chemical states of each element, the metallic state (Al⁰ and Ti⁰) and the oxide state (Al₂O₃ and TiO₂). Obviously, Al₂O₃ and TiO₂ are the main components of the Al 2p and Ti 2p₃/₂ spectrum, respectively. In addition, three peaks were identified in the O 1s spectrum: the first at

text: 3.4. Comparison of the pitting morphologies

Under sufficiently aggressive environments, the integrity and related protective properties of the thin passive film on the alloy surface will be broken down locally, resulting in intensified dissolution at local sites, manifested as pitting, crevice corrosion, and intergranular corrosion [58,65,66]. The characterization of the passive film breakdown can also give a side view of the corrosion resistance of the alloys. Fig. 11 shows the corrosion morphologies of the as-printed and aged Al5Ti5 HEAs undergoing potentiostatic polarization at 1 V $ _{SCE} $ (above the  $ E_{pit} $) for 120 s. As expected, localized corrosion occurs in both alloys in the form of pitting, as shown in the SEM and CLSM images of the as-printed alloy (Fig. 11 (a, b)) and the aged alloy (Fig. 11(d, e)). The corresponding depth profiles (Fig. 11(c, f)) indicate that the pits on the two alloys have approximately equal depths of  $ \sim $30  $ \mu $m. Indeed, stable pits will form above the  $ E_{pit} $ [67]. There has been general agreement that the hydrolysis of metal cations leads to the acidification of the solution within pits, which in turn increases the aggressiveness of the local environment and further leads to the continued dissolution of the pits [65]. The local acidification reactions within pits are (considering a divalent cation obtained by anodic oxidation of a common metal

It is also worth noting that the pits formed on the surface of the as-printed alloy are dense, and most of them are hemispherical in shape. In contrast, pits formed more sparsely on the aged alloy and appeared irregular in shape. Moreover, secondary pits can be observed around the main pit on the aged alloy (Fig. 11(d)). Baroux refers to pits of this feature as semi-developed pits, implying that the pits can continue to develop into more stable pits or repassivate under certain conditions [68].

Accordingly, the dense and large-sized pits formed on the as-printed alloy first indicate its high localized corrosion susceptibility, which is consistent with the polarization tests and EIS analyses. In addition, the changes in pitting morphologies may be related to the potential difference ( $ E_{pit} $ vs. applied potential) and/or the refined grain structure. It is widely accepted that the morphology of pits formed on traditional  $ Cr_{2}O_{3} $-passivated stainless steels is potential-dependent: the etching pits tend to appear at low potentials when the pit surface is in an active state, whereas hemispherical pits are observed at high potentials when the electropolishing (a mode of transpassive dissolution) takes place on the pit surface [71]. 4. Discussion
4.1. STEM analysis of passive films on the aged Al5Ti5 HEA

Considering the significantly improved corrosion resistance exhibited by the aged alloy, the lateral chemical composition and structural aspects of the passive film were analyzed by STEM mapping and atomic-resolution HAADF characterization to gain further insight into the origin of its excellent corrosion resistance. Fig. 12(a) shows a representative STEM-HAADF image of the cross-sectional passive film grown on the aged alloy. Based on the  $ Z^{2} $ relationship between the STEM image intensity and the atomic number, four characteristic layers can be preliminarily identified, which are the Pt layer, Au layer, passive film layer, and alloy matrix from top to bottom [72]. The Au layer with a relatively high atomic number is visualized as a bright white contrast, in sharp contrast to the dark black passive film, which mainly contains oxides and hydroxides with low atomic numbers. The thin passive film was observed to have good adhesion to the alloy matrix, and its thickness was approximately 2.9 nm. The STEM line-scan analysis along the blue arrow in Fig. 12(a) is depicted in Fig. 12(b), and a zoomed-in view of the compositional changes in the vicinity of the passive film is presented in Fig. 12(c). The passive film was found to be significantly enriched in O and Cr, suggesting that the main constituent species are oxides and hydroxides of Cr, which agrees well with the XPS results (Fig. 10(b)). In addition, the Ni and Co content in the passive film was severely depleted relative to the matrix and was replaced by the moderately enriched Al and Ti. The STEM mapping of O, Au, Pt, and individual constituent elements corresponding to Fig. 12(a) confirms the line-scan analyses, as shown in Fig. 12(d). Furthermore, it can be seen that despite the presence of local chemical inhomogeneities in the aged alloy caused by the  $ L_{12} $ precipitation, a continuous and compact passive film can still be formed eventually in the aggressive chloride solution.

Fig. 13 further shows the atomic structure of this passivation-induced surface film. The atomic HAADF image of the cross-sectional passive film observed from the [011] zone axis is presented in Fig. 13(a), which shows the distinguishing structures and contrast features of the thin passive film, Au layer, and alloy matrix. This amorphous passive film is the result of the co-promotion of mixed multiple oxides and hydroxides, suggesting that the passivation reaction between the corrosive solution/passive film/alloy matrix has reached an approximately thermodynamically stable state. Actually, the passive films with an amorphous structure have also been frequently observed at solid-liquid interfaces in various alloy systems, such as the traditional Fe-based SS [73–75], Ni-based alloys [76], Ti alloys [77],  $ Fe_{50}Mn_{30}Co_{10}Cr_{10} $ HEA [78], and Cu nanowires [79]. It is well confirmed that the amorphous structure of passive films can provide an effective barrier to the outward migration of metal cations and the inward migration of corrosive anions due to the

4.2. Mechanisms for the improved corrosion resistance

the aged alloy is shown in Fig. 14(a–d). Under the same corrosive conditions, changes in chemical composition and chemical state ratio of the passive films generally come from changes in alloy compositions and microstructures [65]. Compared with the as-printed alloy, the main changes in the aged alloy are: (i) increased local chemical inhomogeneities due to precipitation of the L1₂ phase (Figs. 4 and 5), (ii) decreased dislocation density (Fig. 3(b)), and (iii) refined grain structure (Fig. 3(a)). It has been reported that galvanic corrosion activities caused by the severely Cr-depleted L1₂ phase could destabilize the passive film, thereby increasing the susceptibility to localized corrosion [28,80]. According to our previous studies, the nano-lamellar L1₂ precipitates are thought to act as cathodic centers, leading to anodic dissolution of the surrounding FCC matrix [28]. In addition, a decrease in the dislocation density of additively manufactured alloys has been reported to slow down the passivation process, resulting in a decrease in the barrier properties of the passive film [81]. For example, the short-term annealing relieved residual stresses in additively manufactured 316 L SS but caused a decrease in pitting resistance, which may be due to the reduction of passivation nucleation sites caused by a decrease in dislocation density [11,81]. If changes (i) and (ii) dominate the electrochemical corrosion behavior of additively manufactured Al5Ti5 HEAs, then the corrosion resistance of the aged alloy would be decreased rather than the improved observed now. This suggests that the change (iii), i.e., grain refinement, greatly affects the passivation process of the aged alloy, which in turn alters the electrochemical corrosion properties. In fact, the grain size refinement can significantly enhance the corrosion resistance of passive metals or alloys, as observed in traditional Al alloys [82,83], Ti alloys [84], Cantor alloys [26,85,86], Al₂Cr₅Cu₅Fe₅₃Ni₃ HEA [87], Ni₆₂Cr₁₀V₂₈ medium-entropy alloy [88]. Grain boundaries, as the most important short-circuit channels, can facilitate the interdiffusion of metals and ions at the alloy/passive film interface, leading to the rapid growth and stabilization of the passive film [82]. Very recently, Jamebozorgi et al. [89] revealed with the help of ReaxFF molecular dynamics simulations that Cr atoms located at or near the grain boundaries of Fe-Cr ferritic SS contribute to the early passivation process by pushing Cr atoms outward from the surface due to their high strain state, which is one of the main driving forces for the formation of the passive films. In the aged Al5Ti5 alloy, the dynamic recrystallization during the DP process leads to grain refinement, and it has been reported that the grain refinement effect caused by DP is more pronounced in pre-strained alloys with high dislocation density [90]. Considering the lower Gibbs formation energies of the Al₂O₃ (-1582.3 kJ/mol), TiO₂ (-888.8 kJ/mol), Cr₂O₃ (-1058 kJ/mol) than that of NiO (-212 kJ/mol) and CoO (-214 kJ/mol) [91], and benefiting from the rapid passivation kinetics brought by the salient grain size refinement, more Al₂O₃ and TiO₂ appear within the Cr₂O₃-dominated passive film on the aged alloy. As beneficial elements for passivation, a moderate increase of Al₂O₃ and TiO₂ within the passive film can promote the protective and robust nature of the passive film, thus enhancing the corrosion resistance of the aged alloy [92–95]. Above all, although the aging treatment leads to an increase in local chemical inhomogeneities as well as a decrease in dislocation density, the grain refinement due to discontinuous precipitation of the L1₂ phase accelerates the passivation process, resulting in an unexpected increase in the anti-corrosion performance of the aged Al5Ti5 HEA.
5. Conclusions

In summary, we have successfully fabricated an additively manufactured HEA,  $ Co_{40}Cr_{20}Ni_{30}Al_{5}Ti_{5} $ (at.%), via the SLM method. The microstructures, electrochemical behavior, and associated passive film characteristics of the novel additively manufactured Al5Ti5HEA in the as-printed and aged states were investigated in detail. The main conclusions are summarized as follows:

(1) Ti-enriched cellular structures (or dislocation cells) with an average size of  $ \sim $400 nm prevailed in the as-printed alloy. The aging treatment partially relieved the residual strain of the as-printed alloy and drove the phase transformation from a single-phase FCC structure to a dual-phase structure of  $ FCC+L1_{2} $. The discontinuous precipitation of  $ L1_{2} $ was identified, which increased the local chemical inhomogeneities of the aged alloy but helped to obtain a finer grain structure. STEM and APT characterizations confirmed that the lamellar  $ L1_{2} $ nanorods were enriched in Ni, Al, and Ti, but depleted in Co and Cr.

(2) The aged alloy exhibited superior corrosion resistance than that of the as-printed counterpart, which was reflected in the increased  $ E_{corr} $,  $ E_{pit} $,  $ R_{p} $, and reduced  $ i_{pass} $ obtained by the potentiodynamic polarization and EIS tests.

(3) The Young model was shown to be the most appropriate model for analyzing the impedance response of the passive films on the surface of both as-printed and aged alloys, revealing an exponential variation of local resistivity through the passive films.

(4) The passive films on both Al5Ti5 HEAs were dominated by  $ Cr_{2}O_{3} $, while relatively more  $ Al_{2}O_{3} $ and  $ TiO_{2} $ were observed in the passive film of the aged alloy. The more protective passive films and related excellent anti-corrosion performance of the aged alloy were primarily attributed to the effect of the refined grain structure on passive film composition and breakdown.

(5) The comparison of the corrosion morphologies between the denser, larger-sized hemispherical pits on the as-printed alloy and the sparse, irregularly shaped pits on the aged alloy once again verified the excellent localized corrosion resistance of the aged alloy.
